question: 4. Prove that if \( A, B \), and \( C \) are angles of a triangle measured in radians then \( A \cos B+\sin A \cos C>0 \).
solution:
Solution. We distinguish two cases.
Case 1. \( 0<B<\pi / 2 \). Then \( \cos B>0 \). Since \( A>0, A>\sin A \); hence \( A \cos B>\sin A \cos B \). Also \( C=\pi-A-B<\pi-B \), and \( 0<C<\pi \), so \( \cos C>\cos (\pi-B)=-\cos B \). Therefore \( \cos B+ \) \( \cos C>0 \). Hence
\[
A \cos B+\sin A \cos C>\sin A(\cos B+\cos C)>0
\]
and the desired inequality is established in Case 1.
Case 2. \( \frac{\pi}{2} \leq B<\pi \). Then \( \cos B \leq 0,0<A<\frac{\pi}{2} \), so \( A<\tan A \), and \( \tan A>0 \). Hence
\[
A \cos B \geq \tan A \cos B .
\]

Also \( B=\pi-A-C \). So
\[
\cos B+\cos A \cos C=-\cos (A+C)+\cos A \cos C=\sin A \sin B>0 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
A \cos B+\sin A \cos C \geq \tan A \cos B+\sin A \cos C \\
\quad=\tan A(\cos B+\cos A \cos C)>0 .
\end{array}
\]

Thus the required inequality is proved in Case 2 also.

descriptive_long.question: 4. Prove that if \( anglealpha, anglebeta \), and \( anglegamma \) are angles of a triangle measured in radians then \( anglealpha \cos anglebeta+\sin anglealpha \cos anglegamma>0 \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We distinguish two cases.
Case 1. \( 0<anglebeta<\pi / 2 \). Then \( \cos anglebeta>0 \). Since \( anglealpha>0, anglealpha>\sin anglealpha \); hence \( anglealpha \cos anglebeta>\sin anglealpha \cos anglebeta \). Also \( anglegamma=\pi-anglealpha-anglebeta<\pi-anglebeta \), and \( 0<anglegamma<\pi \), so \( \cos anglegamma>\cos (\pi-anglebeta)=-\cos anglebeta \). Therefore \( \cos anglebeta+ \) \( \cos anglegamma>0 \). Hence
\[
anglealpha \cos anglebeta+\sin anglealpha \cos anglegamma>\sin anglealpha(\cos anglebeta+\cos anglegamma)>0
\]
and the desired inequality is established in Case 1.
Case 2. \( \frac{\pi}{2} \leq anglebeta<\pi \). Then \( \cos anglebeta \leq 0,0<anglealpha<\frac{\pi}{2} \), so \( anglealpha<\tan anglealpha \), and \( \tan anglealpha>0 \). Hence
\[
anglealpha \cos anglebeta \geq \tan anglealpha \cos anglebeta .
\]

Also \( anglebeta=\pi-anglealpha-anglegamma \). So
\[
\cos anglebeta+\cos anglealpha \cos anglegamma=-\cos (anglealpha+anglegamma)+\cos anglealpha \cos anglegamma=\sin anglealpha \sin anglebeta>0 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
anglealpha \cos anglebeta+\sin anglealpha \cos anglegamma \geq \tan anglealpha \cos anglebeta+\sin anglealpha \cos anglegamma \\
\quad=\tan anglealpha(\cos anglebeta+\cos anglealpha \cos anglegamma)>0 .
\end{array}
\]

Thus the required inequality is proved in Case 2 also.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
<<<
4. Prove that if \( raincloud, stonegate \), and \( windchime \) are angles of a triangle measured in radians then \( raincloud \cos stonegate+\sin raincloud \cos windchime>0 \).
>>>
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
<<<
Solution. We distinguish two cases.
Case 1. \( 0<stonegate<\pi / 2 \). Then \( \cos stonegate>0 \). Since \( raincloud>0, raincloud>\sin raincloud \); hence \( raincloud \cos stonegate>\sin raincloud \cos stonegate \). Also \( windchime=\pi-raincloud-stonegate<\pi-stonegate \), and \( 0<windchime<\pi \), so \( \cos windchime>\cos (\pi-stonegate)=-\cos stonegate \). Therefore \( \cos stonegate+ \) \( \cos windchime>0 \). Hence
\[
raincloud \cos stonegate+\sin raincloud \cos windchime>\sin raincloud(\cos stonegate+\cos windchime)>0
\]
and the desired inequality is established in Case 1.
Case 2. \( \frac{\pi}{2} \leq stonegate<\pi \). Then \( \cos stonegate \leq 0,0<raincloud<\frac{\pi}{2} \), so \( raincloud<\tan raincloud \), and \( \tan raincloud>0 \). Hence
\[
raincloud \cos stonegate \geq \tan raincloud \cos stonegate .
\]

Also \( stonegate=\pi-raincloud-windchime \). So
\[
\cos stonegate+\cos raincloud \cos windchime=-\cos (raincloud+windchime)+\cos raincloud \cos windchime=\sin raincloud \sin stonegate>0 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
raincloud \cos stonegate+\sin raincloud \cos windchime \geq \tan raincloud \cos stonegate+\sin raincloud \cos windchime \\
\quad=\tan raincloud(\cos stonegate+\cos raincloud \cos windchime)>0 .
\end{array}
\]

Thus the required inequality is proved in Case 2 also.
>>>

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 4. Prove that if \( straightness, flatness \), and \( linearity \) are angles of a triangle measured in radians then \( straightness \cos flatness+\sin straightness \cos linearity>0 \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We distinguish two cases.
Case 1. \( 0<flatness<\pi / 2 \). Then \( \cos flatness>0 \). Since \( straightness>0, straightness>\sin straightness \); hence \( straightness \cos flatness>\sin straightness \cos flatness \). Also \( linearity=\pi-straightness-flatness<\pi-flatness \), and \( 0<linearity<\pi \), so \( \cos linearity>\cos (\pi-flatness)=-\cos flatness \). Therefore \( \cos flatness+ \cos linearity>0 \). Hence
\[
straightness \cos flatness+\sin straightness \cos linearity>\sin straightness(\cos flatness+\cos linearity)>0
\]
and the desired inequality is established in Case 1.
Case 2. \( \frac{\pi}{2} \leq flatness<\pi \). Then \( \cos flatness \leq 0,0<straightness<\frac{\pi}{2} \), so \( straightness<\tan straightness \), and \( \tan straightness>0 \). Hence
\[
straightness \cos flatness \geq \tan straightness \cos flatness .
\]

Also \( flatness=\pi-straightness-linearity \). So
\[
\cos flatness+\cos straightness \cos linearity=-\cos (straightness+linearity)+\cos straightness \cos linearity=\sin straightness \sin flatness>0 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
straightness \cos flatness+\sin straightness \cos linearity \geq \tan straightness \cos flatness+\sin straightness \cos linearity \\
\quad=\tan straightness(\cos flatness+\cos straightness \cos linearity)>0 .
\end{array}
\]

Thus the required inequality is proved in Case 2 also.

garbled_string.question: 4. Prove that if \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla \), and \( mncbdjfo \) are angles of a triangle measured in radians then \( qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla+\sin qzxwvtnp \cos mncbdjfo>0 \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We distinguish two cases.
Case 1. \( 0<hjgrksla<\pi / 2 \). Then \( \cos hjgrksla>0 \). Since \( qzxwvtnp>0, qzxwvtnp>\sin qzxwvtnp \); hence \( qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla>\sin qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla \). Also \( mncbdjfo=\pi-qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla<\pi-hjgrksla \), and \( 0<mncbdjfo<\pi \), so \( \cos mncbdjfo>\cos (\pi-hjgrksla)=-\cos hjgrksla \). Therefore \( \cos hjgrksla+ \) \( \cos mncbdjfo>0 \). Hence
\[
qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla+\sin qzxwvtnp \cos mncbdjfo>\sin qzxwvtnp(\cos hjgrksla+\cos mncbdjfo)>0
\]
and the desired inequality is established in Case 1.
Case 2. \( \frac{\pi}{2} \leq hjgrksla<\pi \). Then \( \cos hjgrksla \leq 0,0<qzxwvtnp<\frac{\pi}{2} \), so \( qzxwvtnp<\tan qzxwvtnp \), and \( \tan qzxwvtnp>0 \). Hence
\[
qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla \geq \tan qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla .
\]

Also \( hjgrksla=\pi-qzxwvtnp-mncbdjfo \). So
\[
\cos hjgrksla+\cos qzxwvtnp \cos mncbdjfo=-\cos (qzxwvtnp+mncbdjfo)+\cos qzxwvtnp \cos mncbdjfo=\sin qzxwvtnp \sin hjgrksla>0 .
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla+\sin qzxwvtnp \cos mncbdjfo \geq \tan qzxwvtnp \cos hjgrksla+\sin qzxwvtnp \cos mncbdjfo \\
\quad=\tan qzxwvtnp(\cos hjgrksla+\cos qzxwvtnp \cos mncbdjfo)>0 .
\end{array}
\]

Thus the required inequality is proved in Case 2 also.

kernel_variant.question:
Let A, B, and C be the angles (measured in radians) of a triangle, so that A, B, C are positive and A + B + C = \pi .  Prove the inequality

      A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C > 0.
kernel_variant.solution:
Because A, B, C are angles of a (Euclidean) triangle we have 0 < A, B, C < \pi  and A + B + C = \pi .

We split the proof according to the sign of cos B.


Case 1: cos B \geq  0   (that is, 0 < B \leq  \pi /2).


Step 1.  Compare A and sin A.
For every x in (0, \pi ) one has x > sin x; hence
      A \geq  sin A and therefore A\cdot cos B \geq  sin A\cdot cos B.     (1)
(The inequality is strict unless B = \pi /2.)

Step 2.  Show cos B + cos C is positive.
Since C = \pi  - A - B < \pi  - B, we have 0 < C < \pi  and
      cos C > cos(\pi  - B) = -cos B.
Thus
      cos B + cos C > 0.                                (2)

Step 3.  Combine (1) and (2).
Using (1) and (2) we get
  A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C
     \geq  sin A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C
     = sin A (cos B + cos C)
     > 0.

(In the boundary case B = \pi /2 one has cos B = 0 and the left-hand side equals sin A\cdot cos C = sin A\cdot sin A = sin^2A > 0, so the conclusion still holds.)


Case 2: cos B < 0   (that is, \pi /2 < B < \pi ).


Step 1.  Compare A and tan A.
Because 0 < A < \pi /2, the function x \mapsto  tan x exceeds x, so A < tan A.  Multiplying by the negative quantity cos B reverses the inequality:
      A\cdot cos B \geq  tan A\cdot cos B.                            (3)

Step 2.  Rewrite cos B in terms of A and C.
From B = \pi  - A - C we obtain
      cos B + cos A\cdot cos C
      = -cos(A + C) + cos A\cdot cos C
      = -(cos A\cdot cos C - sin A\cdot sin C) + cos A\cdot cos C
      = sin A\cdot sin C > 0.                               (4)

Step 3.  Combine (3) and (4).
Since sin A\cdot cos C = tan A \cdot  (cos A\cdot cos C),

tan A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C
      = tan A (cos B + cos A\cdot cos C)    (by definition)
      > 0  (by (4) and tan A > 0).

Using (3) we finally get
  A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C
     \geq  tan A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C > 0.


Conclusion.
In both cases the expression A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C is strictly positive; therefore
      A\cdot cos B + sin A\cdot cos C > 0,
as was to be proved.  \blacksquare

core_steps: Partition on the sign of cos B (i.e. B<π/2 vs. B≥π/2).
For an acute angle x, use the elementary bounds  x>sin x  and  x<tan x.
Exploit the triangle relation C=π−A−B to compare cosines:  (i) cos C>−cos B when cos B>0;  (ii) cos B+cos A cos C=sin A sin B>0 when cos B≤0.
Combine the above inequalities so that A cos B+sin A cos C is bounded below by a product of positive quantities in each case.
Conclude A cos B+sin A cos C>0 for all triangle angles.

slot1.description: The actual phrasing of the case split may be given as ‘cos B>0 / cos B≤0’ instead of ‘0<B<π/2 / π/2≤B<π’. The reasoning is unchanged because both descriptions capture the sign of cos B.

slot2.description: The goal could be stated with ≥ instead of >; the proof still delivers the stronger ‘>’ so the chain of arguments is unaffected.
slot2.original: Prove A cos B + sin A cos C > 0
